hcd_data.statement_of_contribution: The property has value as a late-nineteenth- to early-twentieth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian and early-twentieth-century architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, the property is directly associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. Constructed as one of a semi-detached pair, the property retains original building features including its cross-gable roofline, first storey bay window, and fenestration pattern with segmentally arched openings. Contextually, the property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street. The property supports and maintains the residential character of the streetscape and is functionally, visually, and historically linked to the District.

facade_detail.composition: The street elevation spans roughly 5.2 metres across 2 storeys and is capped by a gable, consistent with its house-form, semi-detached typology.
facade_detail.opening_rhythm: The facade keeps a domestic opening rhythm of 2, 2 window bays from lower to upper levels, using double-hung sash windows, and 1 principal entrance.
facade_detail.heritage_summary: Constructed as one of a semi-detached pair, the property retains original building features including its cross-gable roofline, first storey bay window, and fenestration pattern with segmentally arched openings. Contextually, the property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street.

facade_description: 26 Lippincott St presents a house-form, semi-detached facade within the Kensington Market HCD. The street elevation spans roughly 5.2 metres across 2 storeys and is capped by a gable, consistent with its house-form, semi-detached typology. The facade reads primarily as brick, laid in running bond with light grey mortar joints. The facade keeps a domestic opening rhythm of 2, 2 window bays from lower to upper levels, using double-hung sash windows, and 1 principal entrance. HCD note: The property has value as a late-nineteenth- to early-twentieth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian and early-twentieth-century architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, the property is directly associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. Constructed as one of a semi-detached pair, the property retains original building features including its cross-gable roofline, first storey bay window, and fenestration pattern with segmentally arched openings. Contextually, the property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street. The property supports and maintains the residential character of the streetscape and is functionally, visually, and historically linked to the District.
